text: You have completed the Foundation Course. The whole map is yours now — the keyboard, the staff, the note names, and a steady pulse.
From here, the KeyMaster Course begins. This is Key Level One — First Musicianship.
Everything you have learned now starts to become music: reading, rhythm, your hands, harmony, and your first real pieces — with me beside you the whole way.
Every piece of music has a pulse — a steady beat underneath, like a calm, unhurried heartbeat. You can feel it before you play a single note.
A musician feels that pulse first, then places the notes onto it. We will not rush — we feel the beat, and let the music rest on top of it.
Notes do not only tell you which key to play — they tell you how long to hold it. The shape of the note is its length.
A semibreve, the whole note, is held for four beats. A minim, the half note, lasts two. A crotchet, the quarter note, is one beat. The longer notes are hollow; the crotchet is filled in.
Now your first real piece from manuscript — a little tune called Morning Steps. Look at it as a whole before you play.
The right hand rests over five notes, the thumb on C. It steps gently upward, then returns home and settles. Listen to how it sounds.
Music is not only sound. The silences matter just as much — and they are written down too. A written silence is called a rest.
Each note length has a matching rest. A crotchet rest is one beat of silence; a minim rest, two. You count a rest exactly as carefully as a note — the beat keeps going underneath.
Here is a second piece — Blue Window. This one falls gently, like a line drawn downward, then turns and comes home.
The right hand stays in its five-note home, thumb on C. Look at the shape, then listen to how it sounds.
A third piece — First Light. This one teaches a new thing: repeated notes, played gently, then a single skip that opens the melody up.
The right hand stays home over five notes. Listen for the two soft repeats, then the lift up to G.
So far your right hand has done the playing. Now the left hand joins, and it usually reads from the bass clef — the lower of the two clefs.
The bass clef carries the deeper notes, below Middle C. Here is a short left-hand line — notice the fingering counts the other way: the thumb is finger one, on the higher note.
Piano music joins the two clefs into one system — the grand staff. The treble clef on top for the right hand, the bass clef below for the left.
Reading both at once takes care, so we begin very gently: the left hand will hold one steady note while the right hand plays above it.
You may have noticed small numbers beside the notes. Those are fingering — they tell you which finger to use, and they are chosen with care.
Good fingering lets your hand stay calm and reach every note without jumping about. The right finger in the right place is what makes playing feel easy.
The distance between two notes is called an interval, and we name intervals by number. Two notes next-door to each other make a second — that is a step.
Skip a note, and the distance grows to a third — that is a skip. From C, the next-door D is a second; jump over D to E, and that is a third.
You might wonder why we practise patterns at all. Here is why: a pattern trains your fingers to move evenly and independently, so that when a real piece arrives, your hand already knows the shape.
The five-finger pattern hides inside countless melodies. Master it, and you are not just doing an exercise — you are learning the alphabet that pieces are written in.
Every key has a home note — the note the music rests on, where a tune feels finished. Musicians call it the tonic.
In the key of C major, the home note is C. You have felt it already — both your pieces came to rest on C. That feeling of arriving home is the tonic.
This time there is no written music. I will play a short phrase, and you play it straight back to me — by ear alone.
Listen to the shape: where it rises, where it falls. Hold it in your ear, then find it on the keys. Listen carefully now.
Now a sight-reading moment. This is a phrase you have not heard — and I will not play it for you. Reading something new, on your own, is sight-reading.
Look before you play. Find the shape — does it step up or down? Then play it, calmly, in your own time.
